1: For Zion's sake I will not keep silent and for Jerusalem's sake I will not rest — until her righteousness shines out as brightness and her salvation blazes like a torch. The speaker (YHWH or the prophet) commits to persistent, unsilenced advocacy for Jerusalem. The double metaphors of shining brightness and blazing torch are light-intensity images: the righteousness must be unmistakably, brilliantly visible to the watching nations. The refusal to be silent or rest is the posture of the importunate petitioner who does not cease until the answer comes.
2: The nations will see your righteousness and all the kings your glory — you will be called by a new name that the LORD himself will bestow. The new name is a divine act, not a human choice: YHWH himself renames Zion, just as he renamed Abram (Abraham) and Jacob (Israel). The name-gift is an act of identity-creation — the new name defines the new covenant status rather than merely describing it. The nations and kings as witnesses to the new name-giving makes the covenant restoration a public international event.
3: You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of the LORD and a royal diadem in the hand of your God. The crown (ateret tiferet) and royal diadem (tzniph melukhah) are both in YHWH's hand — Zion is displayed as his most prized possession rather than worn on his head. The image is of a jeweler holding up a gem to show its beauty to others. ANE kings wore their crown as the most visible symbol of their majesty; YHWH holds his crown-jewel Zion up for all to see.
4: You will no longer be called Forsaken (Azuvah) nor your land Desolate (Shemamah) — but you will be called My Delight Is in Her (Hephzibah) and your land Married (Beulah); for the LORD delights in you and your land will be wedded. The four names are formal covenant-status designations: Azuvah and Shemamah describe exile's condition; Hephzibah (the actual name of Hezekiah's wife, 2 Kings 21:1) and Beulah describe the restored condition. ANE cities and nations were often named for their political or covenant status; the name-change is simultaneously linguistic and ontological.
5: For as a young man marries a young woman, so your sons will marry you — and as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you. The double marriage metaphor: the sons of Zion are the bridegroom who joyfully inhabits the restored city; YHWH himself is the bridegroom who rejoices over his bride. The word for rejoices (yassis) is the exuberant joy of the wedding celebration — the most festive social event in the ANE calendar. YHWH's rejoicing over Zion is the eschatological climax of the covenant relationship.
6: On your walls, O Jerusalem, I have stationed watchmen — they will never be silent day or night. You who remind the LORD, give yourselves no rest. The watchmen are intercessors, not military sentinels: their job is to never let YHWH forget his promise to restore Jerusalem. The mazkirim (those who remind) is a specific Hebrew term for the royal remembrancer — an official whose duty was to keep the king's obligations before him. These intercessory watchmen are divine appointees keeping YHWH's own promise before him.
7: And give him no rest until he establishes Jerusalem and makes her a glory in the earth. The shockingly bold language of the persistent intercessor: giving YHWH no rest. This inverts the normal logic of petition — the petitioner is usually exhausted before the king responds. Here the intercessors are commanded to persist until the answer comes, and YHWH himself appoints them to this unceasing advocacy. The command mirrors Jesus's parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1-8) — importunate prayer as the appropriate covenantal posture.
8: The LORD has sworn by his right hand and by his strong arm: Never again will I give your grain to your enemies as food, nor will foreigners drink the new wine for which you have labored. The divine oath-gesture — right hand and strong arm — is the most binding self-invocation available in the ANE. YHWH swears by his own power (having nothing greater to swear by, cf. Hebrews 6:13). The content of the oath addresses the specific economic humiliation of tribute: the harvest Israel raised paid foreign tribute-collectors. The oath reverses this permanently.
9: But those who have gathered it will eat it and praise the LORD — and those who bring it in will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary. The covenant meal within the sanctuary precincts is the restoration of the proper social order: the producer eats and praises YHWH; the harvest is consumed in the place of worship rather than in the tribute-halls of foreign emperors. The sanctuary meal is both economic justice and liturgical act — the two are inseparable in the restored covenant community.
10: Pass through, pass through the gates — prepare the way for the people; build up the highway, clear it of stones; raise a banner over the nations. The highway-preparation command recapitulates Isaiah 40:3-4 and Isaiah 57:14. The banner (nes) raised over the nations is the military standard that signals the gathering of the nations to YHWH's cause. Isaiah 11:10-12 also uses the banner-for-nations image as the Davidic signal for the second gathering of Israel's scattered remnant.
11: The LORD has declared to the ends of the earth: Say to daughter Zion, 'See, your salvation is coming — his reward is with him and his recompense before him.' The universal declaration to the ends of the earth echoes Isaiah 40:9-10 and Isaiah 52:7. The salvation arriving with its reward and recompense describes the divine warrior returning from victory: he carries the spoils (sekar) and the distribution (pe'ulah) of his campaign. Revelation 22:12 echoes this: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me.
12: They will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of the LORD — and you will be named Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken. The four titles of the restored community: Holy People (am ha-kodesh), Redeemed of the LORD (ge'ulei YHWH), Sought Out (derushshah), City Not Forsaken (lo ne'ezavah). These names reverse the exile's shaming: the abandoned city becomes the city actively sought; the profane becomes the holy; the unredeemed become the go'el-redeemed. The naming closes the chapter as the culmination of the honor-restoration process.
